title: Mie Goreng Jawa

overview.description: A Javanese-style fried noodle dish made with egg noodles, chicken, cabbage, tomato and a fragrant garlic and shallot spice paste.

method: Prepare the egg noodles according to the packet instructions, taking care not to overcook them.
Add the shallots, garlic, candlenuts if using, ground coriander, ground cumin, chillies if using and salt to a mortar or blender.
Grind or blend to a coarse paste.
Heat the oil in a wok or large frying pan over medium-high heat.
Cook until fragrant and the shallots and garlic have softened.
Add the chicken and stir-fry until almost cooked through.
Push the ingredients to one side of the wok.
Crack in the eggs and scramble until just set.
Add the cabbage and stir-fry for 1–2 minutes.
Add the noodles, kecap manis, light soy sauce and white pepper.
Add the water or chicken stock.
Toss everything together until the noodles are hot and evenly coated.
Add the tomato and spring onions.
Stir-fry briefly until the tomato is warmed and beginning to soften.

learning.did-you-know: Bakmi goreng Jawa is associated particularly with Java and is often cooked to order by street vendors. Javanese versions are generally more aromatic and sweet-savoury than the fiery Malaysian mee goreng.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How a spice paste forms the flavour foundation of a dish and how Javanese cooking balances savoury, aromatic and sweet flavours.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A regional Indonesian noodle dish that gives egg noodles a completely different character from both chow mein and Malaysian mee goreng.
chapman-tips: The bumbu is the heart of the dish, so cook it long enough for the raw garlic and shallot flavour to disappear.
Candlenuts add richness and body but are optional and can be difficult to find.
Keep the chilli moderate in the main dish and serve sambal separately if you want more heat.
Don't overcook the noodles before stir-frying.
The small amount of stock or water helps the noodles absorb the flavour of the bumbu.
Add the tomato near the end so it retains some texture.
Fried shallots are highly recommended; they add sweetness, crunch and a distinctive Indonesian flavour.
notes.batch-cooking: Best eaten fresh. The noodles and vegetables lose texture when frozen and reheated.
notes.leftovers: Reheat quickly in a hot frying pan or wok. Leftovers make a good packed lunch when reheated thoroughly.
